new_classes.definition_en: The berry-like cone of a juniper plant (genus Juniperus), used as the primary flavouring ingredient in gin production.
A distillation process in which rectified cereal spirit or ethyl alcohol is distilled (simply or successively) with juniper berries and other aromatic substances to produce gin.
A spirit obtained by rectification (re-distillation) of a cereal-based fermented mash, used as the base spirit in gin production.

A spirituous beverage obtained by simple or successive distillations of rectified cereal spirits or ethyl alcohol with juniper berries and other aromatic substances (e.g. coriander, angelica root, anise, ginger), classified under heading 2208 50 of the CN.

new_classes.label_de: Gin in Behältnissen mit einem Inhalt von ≤ 2 L (KN 2208 50 11)
new_classes.definition_en: Gin as defined for CN subheadings 2208 50 11 and 2208 50 19, presented in containers holding not more than 2 litres, classified under CN code 2208 50 11.

coverage_explanation: The axioms capture the key distinguishing criteria for CN 2208 50 11: (1) gin as a spirituous beverage obtained by distillation of rectified cereal spirits or ethyl alcohol; (2) flavouring with juniper berries (mandatory) and other aromatic substances; (3) container size ≤ 2 L distinguishing 2208 50 11 from 2208 50 19. A minor gap is that the note specifies that *only* spirituous beverages that meet the gin definition qualify (an exclusivity/sufficiency condition), and the optional use of ethyl alcohol (in addition to rectified cereal spirit) as feedstock is only partially captured — the ethyl alcohol feedstock alternative is not explicitly asserted as a separate restriction. Additionally, the restriction that the beverage must qualify specifically as a "gin" per the legal definition (not merely any juniper-flavoured spirit) is expressed structurally but cannot be fully formalised without additional class definitions for all named aromatic substances. Coverage is therefore set at 0.85.
